event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a title card for 'Oedipus the King, Part 1 of 2,' by Sophocles, adapted by Robin Bond, presented by Western Norway University of Applied Sciences in Bergen 2019, accompanied by a low, sustained, eerie drone. The scene shifts to a dimly lit theater stage under red light, with a small audience flanking a central aisle. Background Performers in period costumes are near the back, including a Performer with Staff. A Woman in Dark Clothing walks down the aisle toward the stage, her silhouette visible against the red light, as the drone intensifies and becomes more dissonant. She reaches the stage and turns to face the group. Another Performer in Light Clothing walks from the stage toward the audience, stopping midway. The red lighting persists, creating a somber atmosphere, now with rhythmic, percussive metallic clang sounds layered over the drone. The Background Performers exhibit minimal movement, observing or waiting, as the metallic strikes increase in frequency and intensity, building to a crescendo. The stage features a White Chair on the left and a Low Platform, contributing to a minimalist, symbolic set design, all within an escalating, resonant soundscape.

Background Performers.description: A group of three or more performers standing near the back of the stage, dressed in period-style garments, appearing to observe or wait.

Woman in Dark Clothing.description: A woman in dark, form-fitting clothing who walks down the central aisle toward the stage, her silhouette prominent under the red lighting.

Performer with Staff.description: A figure standing at the back of the stage holding a long staff, dressed in dark, possibly regal or authoritative attire.

White Chair.description: A simple white wooden chair placed on the left side of the stage, possibly symbolic or functional for the performance.

Low Platform.description: A raised platform or stage extension near the center, where some performers are positioned.

Performer in Light Clothing.description: A performer in lighter, possibly white or beige clothing who walks from the stage toward the audience, pausing mid-way.

raw_segments: The video opens with a title card for 'Oedipus the King, Part 1 of 2,' written by Sophocles and adapted by Robin Bond, presented by Western Norway University of Applied Sciences in Bergen 2019. As a low, sustained, and somewhat eerie drone sound begins, the scene transitions to a dimly lit theater stage bathed in red light, with a small audience seated on either side of a central aisle. On stage, several performers in period-style costumes are positioned near the back, including one holding a staff and others standing near props resembling trees or pillars. A woman in dark clothing walks down the aisle toward the stage, her silhouette stark against the red glow, while the drone continues, maintaining its ominous tone. She reaches the stage and turns, facing the group, as the drone intensifies slightly, becoming more prominent and slightly dissonant. Another performer in lighter clothing walks from the stage toward the audience, pausing mid-way. The lighting remains consistently red, creating a dramatic and somber atmosphere, now accompanied by distinct, rhythmic, almost percussive metallic clang sounds layered over the drone. Minimal movement from the background performers, who appear to be observing or waiting, continues as the metallic strikes become more frequent and intense, building in a crescendo. The stage setup includes a white chair on the left and a low platform, suggesting a minimalist, symbolic set design, all enveloped by the escalating, resonant soundscape.

event_blocks.summary: The scene opens in near darkness, illuminated by a faint greenish light, with the Performer in Red, clad in a long red robe over a white tunic, entering from the left to engage the Woman in Dark Clothing, who stands blindfolded and solemn in a dark, textured hooded robe at center stage. Their interaction is a tense verbal exchange, with the Performer in Red declaring, "I am what I am. A fool, in your opinion, but wise in your parents' eyes," using animated hand gestures, while the Woman in Dark Clothing responds with a questioning tone, "Parents? What parents did I have this very day?" Her solemn, ritualistic movements convey emotional weight. The Performer in Red asserts, "I will give you birth, give you death," before the Woman in Dark Clothing challenges, "Your every single word is cryptic, as if designed to mystify." The Performer in Red, moving toward and briefly sitting on a white wooden chair adorned with flowers on the left, retorts sarcastically, "Well, are you not the best at solving mysteries? Either way, your fate has ruined you, despite your gifts." The Woman in Dark Clothing defiantly states, "As long as I save the city, I do not care," prompting the Performer in Red to declare, "Then I shall go." The Woman in Dark Clothing dismisses them with, "Yes, go! Your presence hinders and obstructs me. Once you are gone, you cannot pain me more." The Performer in Red, rising from the chair, maintains defiance, stating, "I'm not leaving of fear. You have no way of harming me," and begins to deliver a final message, "My message is this: The..." The background remains dim, revealing faint silhouettes of stylized trees and a small house, suggesting a symbolic rural setting, while the audience in the foreground watches intently, some reacting to the unfolding drama. The consistent low, green-tinged lighting throughout enhances the mysterious atmosphere of this intense dialogue.

Background Performers.description: A group of three performers dressed in period-style garments including head coverings, layered fabrics, and earth-toned robes, standing near the right side of the stage, observing the central interaction without significant movement.

Woman in Dark Clothing.description: A woman in a dark, textured hooded robe, blindfolded, standing center stage with her back to the audience initially, then turning to face the Performer in Red, her hands raised in expressive, almost ritualistic gestures, her face partially visible under the hood, conveying solemnity and emotion. She speaks with a questioning, frustrated, defiant, and dismissive tone, uttering lines such as 'What parents did I have this very day?', 'Your every single word is cryptic,' and 'As long as I save the city, I do not care.'

Performer with Staff.description: A figure in dark, hooded, form-fitting clothing, standing at the back right of the stage, holding a long staff with a cross-shaped top, remaining mostly stationary but occasionally shifting weight or gesturing slightly, and receiving a brief touch on the shoulder from the Woman in Dark Clothing; he is identified by the Performer in Red as 'Tiresias,' a blind seer who knows all things and is seen as the city's protector.

White Chair.description: A simple white wooden chair with a cross-back design, placed on the left side of the stage, adorned with a small bouquet of flowers, which the Performer in Red sits on during the scene.

Low Platform.description: A raised platform near the center of the stage, where the Performer in Red and the Woman in Dark Clothing stand during their interaction, serving as the primary focal point of the scene.

Performer in Light Clothing.description: A performer in light-colored, possibly beige or off-white, layered clothing, who briefly interacts with the Woman in Dark Clothing by placing a hand on her shoulder before stepping back.

Performer in Red.description: A performer in a long red robe over a white tunic, with short light-colored hair, who enters from the left, engages in expressive hand gestures toward the Woman in Dark Clothing, moves to a white wooden chair adorned with flowers, sits briefly, then stands again to gesture toward her, maintaining an active, dialogic presence. They speak with a challenging, sometimes sarcastic, powerful, and ultimately defiant tone, delivering lines such as 'I am what I am,' 'I will give you birth, give you death,' and 'You have no way of harming me.'

Audience.description: Silhouetted figures seated in rows of chairs facing the stage, visible in the foreground during the final wide shots, observing the performance with some members turning their heads or raising hands in response to the action.

Stylized Background Set.description: Faint silhouettes of stylized trees and a small house on the right side of the stage, suggesting a rural or symbolic setting, visible in the dimly lit background.

raw_segments: The scene opens in a dimly lit theater bathed in intense red lighting, creating a dramatic and somber atmosphere, underscored by a deep, rhythmic thudding sound that permeates the space. The stage is sparsely set with a white chair on the left and a low platform near the center. A group of performers, including the Performer with Staff holding their staff upright and Background Performers in period-style garments, stand at the back, observing. The Performer in Light Clothing walks from the stage toward the audience, pausing mid-way, as the rhythmic thudding continues. The Woman in Dark Clothing then enters from the audience, her silhouette stark against the red glow, walking down the central aisle toward the stage. As she approaches, the Performer in Light Clothing turns and walks back toward the stage, meeting the Woman in Dark Clothing near the low platform for a brief, intense interaction, where the Performer in Light Clothing appears to gesture or speak to her. The rhythmic thudding persists, maintaining the tension. The camera zooms in on this intimate and emotionally charged interaction, with the Performer in Light Clothing now comforting or guiding the Woman in Dark Clothing, placing a hand on her shoulder. The red lighting amplifies the tension, while the Background Performers remain in the shadows, silently watching. Towards the end of the minute, the rhythmic thudding intensifies and quickens, accompanied by a metallic clanging, suggesting a more frantic and powerful percussive action, likely originating from the Performer with Staff, as the White Chair remains untouched, a silent witness to the unfolding drama.

The scene opens in near darkness, illuminated only by red and blue stage lights, with silhouettes of performers moving across the stage, accompanied by a rhythmic, percussive sound, like a drum or large object being struck. As the lighting gradually brightens, the full stage becomes visible, featuring a black curtain backdrop, a white chair adorned with flowers on the left, and a low platform near center stage. A deep, resonant gong sound punctuates the atmosphere. A Performer in a long red robe, later identified as Oedipus, enters from the left, walking toward the center. A Performer with Staff, in dark, hooded, form-fitting clothing, stands motionless at the back right, holding a long staff. The Performer in Red approaches the Performer with Staff, gesturing with open hands as if in conversation or confrontation, and speaks with a commanding, theatrical male voice, asking, "My children, oh what is it that you want?" Meanwhile, on the right side of the stage, a group of Background Performers in period-style garments remain mostly stationary, observing. One of them, the Performer in Light Clothing, briefly interacts with the Woman in Dark Clothing by placing a hand on her shoulder before stepping back. The Woman in Dark Clothing, in a dark hooded robe, stands near the Performer with Staff, occasionally shifting her stance. The Performer with Staff, speaking with a slightly lower, more somber male voice, responds, "The city," elaborating that it is "filled alike with the smoke of sacrifice and cries of misery." The Performer in Red then identifies himself, stating, "My children, I have come myself. I, Oedipus." He then addresses the Performer with Staff, saying, "Now then, speak, old man, since you are the natural spokesman for these folk," and asks if they have gathered "in dread or in need," offering his help. The Performer with Staff replies, addressing him as "Lord Oedipus," and begins to speak of "requests." The White Chair remains untouched, and the Low Platform serves as the focal point of this dramatic, theatrical interaction, with lighting focused on the central figures.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black backdrop, where a central interaction takes place on a low platform. The Performer in Red, dressed in a flowing red robe over a white tunic, walks from the left toward the center, gesturing expressively with open hands as he delivers a solemn and pleading speech. He describes a city in distress, stating, "the city is at sea and can no longer lift her head above the fatal waves," and warns that "a deadly plague is upon the town," with "Hell's black halls... rich with cries of grief and pain." He addresses the Performer with Staff as "my Lord," thanking him for saving their lives from "cruel sins" and asserting that "a city is worth nothing without its citizens." Throughout this, the Performer with Staff, clad in dark hooded attire and holding a staff topped with a cross, stands mostly motionless, maintaining a solemn presence, occasionally shifting weight and gesturing subtly. To the right, the Woman in Dark Clothing stands near the Performer with Staff, her posture shifting slightly as she observes the exchange. The Performer in Light Clothing briefly places a hand on her shoulder before stepping back, suggesting a moment of comfort or connection. Nearby, the Background Performers remain mostly still, observing the central interaction, dressed in earth-toned, layered garments with head coverings. The White Chair on the left, adorned with a small bouquet, remains untouched. As the Performer in Red concludes his plea, the Performer with Staff responds with a deeper, authoritative voice, declaring, "I pity you, my children. I know well why you have come here. And what you want. I know..." The lighting remains consistent, focused on the central figures, while the audience silhouettes are visible in the foreground. The scene ends with a sudden dimming of lights, fading the stage into darkness.

The scene opens on a dimly lit stage, where a group of performers in period-style costumes are arranged. The Performer in Red, wearing a flowing red robe, walks from the left toward the center, approaching the Performer with Staff, who stands motionless on a low platform holding a cross-topped staff. The Performer in Red speaks with a dramatic, commanding male voice, declaring, "there is not one of you whose sickness matches mine," and then reveals his plan for safety, stating he has sent "my wife's very own brother, Creon." The camera zooms in on their interaction as the Performer with Staff, in dark hooded clothing, responds with a more deferential male voice, addressing the Performer in Red as "my lord" and confirming Creon's imminent arrival, adding, "His news are good, it seems." The Performer in Red expresses hope that Creon comes "with luck and saving grace," then calls out, "Creon, what news have you come to bring us?" A Woman in Dark Clothing observes nearby, briefly placing a hand on the Performer with Staff’s shoulder before stepping back, while Background Performers remain mostly still on the right. A new, distinct male voice, identified as Creon, replies, "Oh, the news are good, my lord. All our bad luck will be reversed and changed for the good, if things are done right," prompting the Performer in Red to question, "Done right?" The solemn, theatrical atmosphere is maintained by the focused lighting and slow, deliberate pace.

The scene opens on a dimly lit stage, where multiple performers are positioned. The Performer in Red, wearing a long flowing red robe over a white tunic, walks from the left toward the center, engaging in a dialogue with the Performer with Staff, who stands at the back right in dark hooded attire holding a staff with a cross-shaped top. The Performer in Red, speaking with a commanding and expressive voice, initiates the conversation by questioning, "Creon, what kind of talk is this?" and later asks about the fate of "King Laius." The Performer with Staff, identified as Creon, responds in a calmer, informative tone, addressing the Performer in Red as "my lord," explaining that an oracle demands banishing "the source of pollution from land" and driving out "a murderer," further revealing that "King Laius" was killed by bandits in Thebes. The Performer in Red gestures expressively while speaking, at one point sitting on the White Chair adorned with flowers, before standing again and gesturing toward Creon. The Woman in Dark Clothing, in a dark hooded robe, stands near Creon, briefly placing a hand on their shoulder before stepping back, while the Performer in Light Clothing interacts similarly with the Woman in Dark Clothing before retreating. Background Performers, dressed in earth-toned period garments, observe from the right. The lighting remains focused on the central figures on the Low Platform, emphasizing the intense exchange as the Performer in Red declares, "I will open up this case once more from scratch... and you shall all see that I will join in taking revenge." The audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground.

The scene opens on a dimly lit, sparsely decorated stage, where the Performer in Red, wearing a long red robe over a white tunic, walks dramatically from the left toward the center. With expressive gestures, he engages in a powerful, theatrical monologue, declaring in a commanding voice, 'Everyone, Oedipus has just now promised us the object of our prayers and our journey here.' He addresses the 'citizens of Thebes,' stating, 'I make this declaration,' and demanding information about King Laius's murder, as he has 'inherited the power King Laius used to hold before.' While he speaks, moving toward a Low Platform and then back, the Performer with Staff stands motionless in dark hooded attire, holding a cross-topped staff, receiving the Performer in Red's intense focus. To the right, three Background Performers in earth-toned period garments observe silently. The Woman in Dark Clothing, near the Performer with Staff, briefly places a hand on their shoulder, followed by the Performer in Light Clothing touching the Woman in Dark Clothing's shoulder. The Performer in Red then moves to a flower-adorned White Chair on the left, sits briefly, and stands again, continuing his impassioned speech, asserting his role as King Laius's successor and declaring, 'I shall be his champion!' The focused stage lighting highlights the central figures, emphasizing the dramatic and theatrical atmosphere of his declaration.

The scene opens with the Performer in Red, clad in a flowing red robe over a white tunic, walking from the left side of the stage toward the center, gesturing expressively as he delivers a commanding monologue in a dramatic male voice, declaring, "if you were... well, yes, in fact, my very own father. And I will leave no stone unturned. As for the rest of you who find my words agreeable, may justice fight for you." As he moves, the camera pulls back to reveal a wider view of the dimly lit stage, set against a dark curtain backdrop. On the right, the Performer with Staff—a figure in dark hooded clothing holding a staff with a cross-shaped top—stands mostly stationary, accompanied by the Woman in Dark Clothing, who briefly places a hand on the Performer with Staff’s shoulder. The Performer in Light Clothing, dressed in beige layers, interacts with the Woman in Dark Clothing before stepping back. The Performer in Red then turns and gestures toward the Performer with Staff. A clear female voice, belonging to the Woman in Dark Clothing, responds, "Since you, my lord, have put me under oath, I shall speak. I did not kill the king, nor do I know who did." The Performer in Red's male voice replies, "Your words are just." The Woman in Dark Clothing then states, "I do know a lordly man. Tiresias," a name the Performer in Red repeats with growing emphasis. The lighting shifts subtly, focusing on the central figures, while Background Performers in earth-toned period garments observe from the right. The Performer in Red, now sitting on the White Chair adorned with flowers, then rises again to gesture toward the Performer with Staff, addressing him directly: "You know all things. Blind though as you are. You sense our city's plight and its disease. In you alone, Tiresias, we find a man to stand as our protector." The scene concludes with a dramatic shift in perspective: the camera moves to the audience’s viewpoint, showing the Performer in Red illuminated by a spotlight, while the Woman in Dark Clothing walks toward the audience, her back to the camera, and raises her hand in a gesture that suggests a farewell or invocation. The White Chair and Low Platform remain visible on the dimly lit stage, with the audience silhouetted in the foreground.

The scene opens in near darkness, with a spotlight illuminating a Woman in Dark Clothing, clad in a textured, dark hooded robe, standing with her back to the audience, hands slightly raised in supplication. As the lighting shifts to a greenish hue, she slowly turns, revealing a blindfold beneath her hood, her movements deliberate and expressive, hands gesturing as if feeling her way. Simultaneously, a Performer in Red, wearing a long red robe over a white tunic, enters from the left, approaching with open, expressive hand gestures, engaging in a dramatic monologue. He speaks with a commanding, theatrical voice, declaring, "Freedom will only and only come if we with certainty find and identify the men who murdered the king. And kill them too. Or drive them, exile them away from this land." The two stand on a low platform, facing each other, with the Performer in Red occasionally stepping back and gesturing towards the Woman in Dark Clothing, whose blindfolded face is partially visible under her hood, maintaining a solemn posture. He continues, his tone shifting to a more pleading or questioning note, "Please, Oedipus, send me home. Is it, is it by far for you to bear your fate? For me to bear mine, if you obey." The lighting intensifies on the Woman in Dark Clothing, highlighting her robe's texture. The Performer in Red then moves to a white wooden chair adorned with flowers on the left, sits briefly, and rises again, gesturing towards her. He then becomes accusatory, stating, "You speak words out of keeping and unfriendly to the state which raised you by refusing to interpret your will. I see that what you say is far from fortunate. And I hold my peace to obviate the risk of suffering the same." The Woman in Dark Clothing continues her expressive hand gestures, sometimes touching her chest or raising them dramatically, while the Performer in Red responds with animated gestures, creating a ritualistic exchange. The camera occasionally zooms in on her, emphasizing her blindfolded state and emotional weight. The Performer in Red's voice rises to a forceful, almost shouting demand, "No! You will not turn away from us! If you have knowledge and we all kneel in supplication..." The scene concludes with both figures still engaged, the Performer in Red gesturing towards her, and the audience visible in silhouette in the foreground, some raising hands in response, indicating a live theatrical context.

The scene unfolds in near-total darkness, illuminated by a greenish spotlight that casts an eerie glow on the central figures. The Performer in Red, dressed in a long red robe over a white tunic, enters from the left, immediately engaging in a heated verbal exchange with animated hand gestures. This performer, speaking with an exasperated, questioning, and outraged tone, approaches the Woman in Dark Clothing, who stands center stage blindfolded in a textured, dark hooded robe, raising her hands in expressive, ritualistic gestures. The Performer in Red exclaims, "I cannot believe you will not speak what you know," and later, "This is an outrage! You will provoke a very storm." The Woman in Dark Clothing, whose face is partially visible under her hood, conveys solemnity and emotion as she responds with a defiant and refusing tone, stating, "Fools, all of you! I shall never reveal the cause of my distress," and "There is nothing to be gotten from me." As the Performer in Red moves closer and then steps back, the intense dialogue continues, with the Woman in Dark Clothing defiantly asserting, "You criticize my temper, my lord, but you do not know your own private situation. Yourself, the land's pollution," to which the Performer in Red retorts, "How dare you!" The Performer in Red then moves to a white wooden chair adorned with flowers on the left, sits briefly while accusing, "I say, maybe you are the murderer of him whose murder you seek," then stands again to gesture toward the Woman in Dark Clothing, who turns slightly, maintaining her blindfolded state and continuing her expressive movements. The Performer in Red concludes with a threatening tone, "Then it would seem to me that the both of you will regret your..." The background remains dim, with faint silhouettes of stylized trees and a small house visible on the right, suggesting a rural or symbolic setting. The audience, visible in the foreground during the final wide shots, observes the performance with some members turning their heads or raising hands in response to the tense and theatrical action, enhanced by the mysterious green lighting.

The scene unfolds in near-total darkness, illuminated by a greenish spotlight that casts an eerie glow on the central figures. The Woman in Dark Clothing, blindfolded and draped in a textured green hooded robe, stands with her back to the audience before turning to face the Performer in Red, her hands raised in expressive, ritualistic gestures. The Performer in Red, clad in a long red robe over a white tunic, enters from the left, immediately engaging in an intense verbal confrontation. The Performer in Red's voice is aggressive and commanding, initially demanding, "hands to purge me from this land," then questioning, "Who are your parents, huh? Do you know?" and delivering a curse: "The curse of your father and your mother will drive your injured feet in exile from this land." The Woman in Dark Clothing responds with a firm, defensive tone, asserting, "Although you are the king, I have the right to answer back," and later, "You may have your sight, but you do not see the evil you are." As they move across a Low Platform, the Performer in Red gestures emphatically, shouting, "Damn you! Damn you to hell! Be gone and swiftly! Leave this house!" The Woman in Dark Clothing responds with subtle shifts in posture and hand movements, stating, "I wouldn't have come here if you hadn't called for me." The Performer in Red then moves to a White Chair adorned with flowers, sits briefly, then rises to continue the interaction, concluding the exchange with a furious, "And I would never have called you here if I would have known you would spout such foolishness!" The Stylized Background Set, featuring faint silhouettes of trees and a small house, remains visible in the dim background, reinforcing a symbolic atmosphere, while the audience, visible in the foreground during wider shots, watches intently, some turning their heads or raising hands in reaction to the escalating drama. The lighting remains consistently low and green-tinged, enhancing the mysterious and theatrical mood throughout this heated exchange.

event_blocks.summary: In near darkness under faint greenish-blue stage lighting, a Performer in Red, clad in a long red robe over a white tunic, stands on a low platform and dramatically declares, "The one you are looking for, the one you claim killed Laios, that man is here. A foreigner, no resident. In fact, he will emerge as a true-born son of Thebes." Facing him, a blindfolded Woman in Dark Clothing, in a shimmering hooded robe, initially has her back to the audience. As the Performer continues, "Now, go inside and think of this. And if you ever find out that I lied, you can indeed say that I know nothing about prophecy," she turns to face him, raising her hands in expressive, ritualistic gestures with solemnity. The Performer in Red responds with subtle hand movements. As his speech concludes, lighting intensifies, revealing a white chair with flowers and stylized tree and house silhouettes, while a low, sustained, atmospheric droning sound begins. The Woman in Dark Clothing slowly walks forward, stepping off the platform with outstretched arms, accompanied by the drone. The Performer in Red watches her, then sits briefly on the white chair before standing and gesturing toward her. The audience, visible as silhouettes, watches intently. As the Woman in Dark Clothing reaches the front, a high-pitched metallic resonance briefly joins the drone before fading. She pauses, turns, and walks back toward the platform. The lighting dims, focusing on the Performer in Red, who stands alone with clasped hands, as the Woman in Dark Clothing exits. The stage plunges into near darkness with faint blue lights, and the droning sound slowly fades out.

He speaks in a clear, dramatic male voice, delivering a prophecy about 'the one you are looking for' and 'a true-born son of Thebes,' claiming knowledge of prophecy.

event_blocks.summary: The scene opens on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, a stone-arch prop, and a white chair adorned with flowers. The Performer in Red, in a red tunic and black leggings, confronts a group of Background Performers, declaring accusations against Euripus the King and stating he wishes no longer to live if found guilty. A woman among the Background Performers suggests the charges might stem from rage, not reason. As this dialogue occurs, the Second Performer in Red, in a long red robe, enters and approaches the chair. The Performer in Red then aggressively questions the Second Performer in Red, identified as Creon, about his return despite being convicted of King Laius's murder and his alleged attempt to seize the throne. Creon pleads to be heard before judgment. Later, the Performer in Red, identified as Oedipus, demands the Second Performer in Red prove his innocence regarding Laius's death, questioning the duration since Laius died and the investigation into the homicide. The Second Performer in Red denies being a murderer and confirms taking Oedipus's sister as his wife. Oedipus asks Creon's intentions, to which Creon chillingly declares he wants Oedipus's death, not banishment. A female voice from the Background Performers pleads for them to stop. The Woman in White Gown, with red trim and a red headband, mediates between Oedipus and the Second Performer in Red, urging them to cease their conflict. After a moment of connection where the Second Performer in Red places a hand on Oedipus's shoulder, Oedipus concedes, and the Second Performer in Red agrees to leave, noting Oedipus's ignorance but the woman's justice. The Woman in White Gown then questions Oedipus about his rage. The Second Performer in Red accuses Oedipus's "husband" (Oedipus) of threatening him, while Oedipus asserts Creon's guilt in plots against his life. The Woman in White Gown recounts a prophecy that King Laius would be killed by his son, born by her, but that Laius died by foreign bandits, suggesting the prophecy was not fulfilled. The Second Performer in Red, Oedipus, questions the location where the king was killed, specifically if it was at a three-road intersection. The Woman in White Gown confirms the location and reveals that a murder was announced just before Oedipus took control of the land. Oedipus, distressed, asks about King Laius's appearance, and the Woman in White Gown describes him as a big man, not much different from Oedipus, causing Oedipus to cry out in anguish about dreadful curses. He asks if Laius was escorted by soldiers or a few men, and the Woman in White Gown states he had five men and an officer, information relayed by a slave who escaped and later begged to be sent to the fields upon Oedipus's rise to power. Oedipus urgently requests to see the slave, and the Woman in White Gown reassures him he will come. The Second Performer in Red then reveals his parents were King Polybus and Queen Merope, and that an oracle foretold he was not his father's son, prompting him to travel to Delphi where an oracle delivered a prophecy.

Background Performers.description: A group of three performers in period-style, earth-toned garments with head coverings, standing near stylized tree and house props on the right side of the stage, observing the central interaction; one bends forward slightly during the scene.

Second Performer in Red.description: A performer in a long red robe over a white tunic, with short light-colored hair, who engages in expressive dialogue with the Woman in White Gown. He speaks with a dramatic male voice, narrating a personal story about his parents, King Polybus and Queen Merope, and a past incident where his parentage was questioned, leading him to travel to Delphi to consult an oracle. He expresses worry, anger, and a confessional tone through his speech and gestures, extending his hand toward the Woman in White Gown in a moment of connection before bowing his head.

Performer in Red.description: A performer in a shorter red tunic over black leggings, with short light-colored hair, who listens and responds to the Second Performer in Red with subtle gestures, standing center stage during their interaction. He speaks with an assertive male voice, accusing the Second Performer in Red of 'evil plots' and is implicitly named 'Oedipus' by him, eventually conceding to 'let him go'.

Audience.description: Silhouetted figures seated in rows of chairs facing the stage, visible in the foreground during wider shots, observing the performance with some members turning their heads or raising hands in response to the action.

Stylized Background Set.description: Faint silhouettes of stylized trees and a small house on the right side of the stage, suggesting a rural or symbolic setting, visible in the dimly lit background.

White Chair.description: A simple white wooden chair with a cross-back design, placed beside the stone-arch prop, adorned with a small bouquet of flowers, and used by the Second Performer in Red as a seat during the interaction.

Woman in White Gown.description: A woman in a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, standing near a stone-arch prop, engaging in expressive dialogue with the Second Performer in Red, using hand gestures and shifting her stance to convey emotion, and reciprocating his gesture by reaching out to him, all while listening intently with a concerned expression.

Stone-arch Prop.description: A gray, arched structure resembling a stone doorway, positioned center stage, serving as a symbolic backdrop for the interaction between the Woman in White Gown and the Second Performer in Red.

Hand with Phone on Stick.description: A hand holding a smartphone mounted on a stick, entering from the left side of the frame, capturing the performance, adding a modern, audience-participation element to the scene.

Colorful Fabric Props.description: Brightly colored fabric arrangements resembling flowers or foliage, placed on low platforms at the front of the stage, adding visual contrast to the dark setting and framing the central performance area.

raw_segments: The scene opens in near darkness, gradually revealing a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop. As the lights rise, a Performer in Red, in a vibrant red tunic and black leggings, stands center stage. He addresses a group of Background Performers on the right, dressed in earth-toned, period-style garments, declaring in a strong, declarative male voice, "My fellow citizens, I'm hearing that Euripus the King is making dreadful accusations against me. If anyone can find that I've caused him any kind of hurt, well, then I have no further wish to live." He gestures expressively, particularly engaging with a woman in a white blouse and headscarf among the Background Performers. She responds calmly in a female voice, "Perhaps the charges he made against you came from rage rather than reason." The Performer in Red questions, "Then what I hear is true. He is quite convinced," to which she clarifies, "He did say that, but I do not know if it is true." He presses further, "And did he really make these accusations against me?" and she hesitantly replies, "I do not know." As this dialogue unfolds, another figure, the Second Performer in Red, in a long red robe over a white tunic, enters from the left, approaching a white wooden chair adorned with flowers beside a stone-arch prop. The first Performer in Red then turns to him, his voice becoming aggressive and commanding, accusing, "You there! My lord. How have you dared come back here, although being convicted of King Laius's murderer? And clearly, you seek to rob me of my throne. Tell me, Creon, what stupidity do you see in me to plot these crimes?" The Second Performer in Red, now identified as Creon, responds calmly and pleadingly in a male voice, "My lord, please, observe my lips and listen to my reply before you judge, please." The Background Performers remain mostly stationary, observing the dramatic exchange, with one bending slightly forward. The lighting remains focused on the central figures, creating a dramatic contrast, while the audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground during wider shots, indicating a live theatrical performance.

The video opens with a wide shot of a dimly lit stage, where a dramatic performance unfolds before a seated, silhouetted audience. Center stage, two performers in red attire engage in an intense dialogue. The Performer in Red, in a shorter red tunic over black leggings, initiates the confrontation with a deep, authoritative voice, declaring, "Just let me explain this charge. I charge you to demonstrate your innocence." He gestures expressively as he questions the Second Performer in Red, who wears a long red robe over a white tunic, about the death of Laius, demanding, "How long has it been since Laius died? Did you not investigate the homicide?" The Second Performer in Red, initially defensive with a slightly higher voice, responds, "Just tell me. What is it that you think I've done wrong?" and later, "We held inquiries, of course we did. But there were no success." As the camera zooms in, highlighting their interaction against a black curtain, the Second Performer in Red vehemently denies, "Still, I will not be proven a murderer!" The Performer in Red then shifts his accusation, challenging, "But is it not true that you have taken my sister to be your wife and the mother of your children?" to which the Second Performer in Red confidently replies, "Of course. Why would I deny the obvious?" The camera pulls back to reveal three Background Performers in earth-toned, period-style garments standing near stylized tree and house props on the right, observing the escalating exchange, one bending forward in attentiveness. On the left, a White Chair adorned with flowers sits beside a stone-arch prop. The Performer in Red, asserting his position, asks, "So what is it that you want? To drive me exile from this land?" The Second Performer in Red turns toward him, placing a hand on his shoulder in a moment of chilling connection, and declares with shocking intensity, "Oh no. It is your death that I want, not your banishment!" The Performer in Red, now identified by name as Oedipus, defiantly retorts, "But I have a share in this city as well, Oedipus. It is not yours alone." The dramatic tension is abruptly interrupted by a female voice from the Background Performers, pleading, "Please, stop my lords! I..." The lighting remains focused on the central figures, emphasizing the emotional weight of their conflict, with the red costumes stark against the dark stage, underscoring themes of passion, power, and impending tragedy.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, where a central narrative develops between three main performers. The Second Performer in Red, wearing a long red robe over a white tunic, enters from the left as a stern female voice commands, "You foolish man... Go home." He approaches the Performer in Red, dressed in a shorter red tunic, and they engage in a tense dialogue. The Second Performer in Red, speaking with a distressed male voice, accuses "your husband" (the Performer in Red) of threatening to drive him from the land or murder him. The Performer in Red, with an assertive male voice, retorts, "Exactly! As I have found him guilty in evil plots against my life." A woman in a flowing white gown, adorned with a red headband, joins them, standing between the two performers in red and gesturing as if mediating. She speaks with the stern female voice, urging, "Leave him, or the abyss, please. Respect him and have respect for me as well." In a moment of connection or reassurance, the Second Performer in Red places a hand on the Performer in Red’s shoulder. The Performer in Red then concedes, stating, "Then yes. Let him go." The Second Performer in Red, accepting, declares, "I will go," adding, "You, Oedipus, I find ignorant, but she, she has found me just." Meanwhile, on the right, Background Performers in earth-toned garments observe near stylized tree and house props, with one bending forward slightly. The White Chair, decorated with flowers, remains stationary on the left beside a stone-arch prop. The camera gradually zooms in on the central trio, while the audience in the foreground is visible as silhouettes, some turning their heads or raising hands in response. The Woman in White Gown then addresses the Performer in Red, asking, "My lord, what is the reason of your rage?" The lighting remains focused on the central performers, enhancing the dramatic atmosphere.

The scene opens on a dimly lit stage, featuring a simple stone-arch prop and a white cross-back chair adorned with flowers, all set against a black curtain backdrop. A woman in a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, the Woman in White Gown, stands near the arch, facing a young man in a long red robe over a white tunic, the Second Performer in Red. The Second Performer in Red initiates a dramatic dialogue with an accusatory and strong voice, declaring, "The reason, the reason is your brother and his plots against me!" He gestures emphatically and shifts his stance. The Woman in White Gown responds calmly, asking, "Can you tell me clearly, who broke the conflict?" The Second Performer in Red, sounding defensive, states, "He claims I am guilty of King Laius's death," then bows his head in apparent sorrow or contemplation. When she asks if the information is first-hand, he replies scornfully, "Oh, he sent some wretched prophet!" and adds sarcastically, "So, now, he's free of blame!" The Woman in White Gown, with subtle hand movements and a shifting gaze conveying emotional depth, then takes a more authoritative tone, telling him, "Well then. Set yourself free from charges of this, and hear me out." The Second Performer in Red moves to sit on the white chair, and the Woman in White Gown approaches him, placing a hand on his shoulder in a gesture of comfort or connection, holding hands briefly. She steps back, continuing to speak with expressive gestures and a confident voice, asserting, "No mortal man can share divination. And I will reveal a simple proof of this." She then recounts a narrative, stating, "An oracle came to King Laius once and said that it will be his fate to die by the hands of his son, born by me. But, according to the report, King Laius died by the hand of foreign bandits." The lighting remains focused on the two, casting soft shadows, while the audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground during wider shots, observing the intimate and emotionally tense exchange.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, centered around a gray stone-arch prop. The Woman in White Gown, wearing a flowing off-the-shoulder white gown with red trim and a red headband, stands near the arch, engaging in expressive dialogue. She speaks with a clear, dramatic female voice, recounting a prophecy about a place where three roads meet and a son who was three days old, stating, "The faith did not ensure that the boy should be his father murder." She continues, addressing the seated Second Performer in Red, "You, Oedipus, have no part in them." The Second Performer in Red, dressed in a long red robe over a white tunic, sits on a white wooden chair adorned with a small bouquet of flowers, listening intently. He then interrupts with an urgent, questioning male voice, exclaiming, "No, wait, wait, my wife! I thought what I heard was this: The place where the king was cut down was where three roads meet. This spot where the crime took place. Where is it?" As he speaks, he gestures with his hands and bows his head, while the Woman in White Gown moves gracefully, turning and gesturing, conveying emotion through her posture and facial expressions. She steps closer, placing a hand on his shoulder in a moment of connection before stepping back slightly, maintaining eye contact, as their intimate and dramatic exchange unfolds, emphasizing their presence against the dark background. The audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground, observing the performance.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop and a gray stone-arch prop, where the Woman in White Gown, in a flowing white gown with red trim, stands. The Second Performer in Red, identified as Oedipus, clad in a long red robe, begins seated on a white chair adorned with flowers, engaging her in an intense dialogue. The Woman in White Gown calmly states, "land is called for kiss. The road leads two places from Delphi and from Dalea." Oedipus, with an urgent tone, questions, "And how much time has passed since then?" She then reveals, "A murder was announced in town, but just before you name control of the land." Visibly distressed, Oedipus rises, gesturing emphatically and bowing his head, exclaiming loudly, "No! It cannot be!" The Woman in White Gown, now addressing him as "Oedipus," asks with concern, "What is it that weighs on your soul?" Oedipus, in turn, demands, "No questions yet. My wife, tell me first," confirming their relationship, before intensely asking, "What sort of a man was King Lions?" She calmly responds, addressing him as "my lord," "A big man, my lord. Not much different from you." This revelation causes Oedipus to cry out in anguish, "No! There are dreadful curses upon me this day!" He moves closer to her, extending his hand toward her in a gesture of connection, which she reciprocates, their eye contact filled with subtle, emotive expressions. The Woman in White Gown, concerned, asks, "What do you mean?" Oedipus, his voice now filled with dread and pleading, states, "There is dread in my heart, that the sight of the prophet might be true. Please, tell me just one more thing. Was he escorted by a few or did he have soldiers besides him?" The audience is visible in silhouette, observing the performance as the dramatic tension builds between the central figures.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, centered around a stone-arch prop and a white cross-back chair adorned with a small bouquet. The Woman in White Gown, dressed in a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, stands near the chair, engaging in a dramatic dialogue. She speaks with a calm, clear female voice, narrating, "He had a party of five. And an officer with them. Lies wrote in a single card." The Second Performer in Red, wearing a long red robe over a white tunic, begins by adjusting his robe, then interjects with a strong, questioning male voice, "And who was it that told you this, my queen?" The Woman in White Gown responds, "A slave. Who escaped and came back home." The Second Performer in Red, gesturing expressively, presses, "This slave. Is he still in this house now?" She continues her narration, explaining, "Why no? When he came back, and saw you with power, and Lies being dead, he came to me. He begged me to send him to the fields as he would be rather herding sheep than being teased. So I sent him there." He bows his head in a moment of emotional weight before extending his hand toward her, asking urgently, "Well, how can we get him back?" She responds simply, "By Tinsley." He questions, "But why would he be doing that?" then quickly shifts, "I, I fear I have said too much. I wish to see him now, my wife!" The Woman in White Gown responds with subtle hand movements and shifts her stance, reciprocating his gesture by reaching out to him, reassuring him, "He will come, my lord." She begins to speak again, "But I am...", as the segment ends. The stage floor is decorated with colorful, crumpled paper resembling flowers, and in the foreground, silhouettes of the Audience are visible, some turning their heads or raising hands, with a hand holding a phone on a stick briefly entering from the left to capture the scene.

The dimly lit stage, set against a black curtain backdrop, features a symbolic stone-arch prop and a white cross-back chair adorned with flowers. The Woman in White Gown, in a flowing off-white gown with red trim and a red headband, stands near the arch, engaged in a dramatic and emotional exchange with the Second Performer in Red, who wears a long red robe over a white tunic. The Second Performer in Red, speaking with a dramatic male voice, begins by stating, "I shall not keep it from you," despite being "truly, truly worried." He extends his hand, which the Woman in White Gown reciprocates by briefly holding it, before he lowers his head in sorrow, releasing her hand and clasping his own. As he continues to gesture expressively, sometimes placing his hands on his chest, he reveals, "My father was King Polybus. My mother, Queen Merope." He recounts how "a man, well-gone in drinking, claimed I was not my father's proper son," leading to his anger and his parents' outrage. He then explains, "Still, I traveled to Delphi, and there an oracle..." The Woman in White Gown remains mostly still, her posture conveying attentiveness and emotional weight as she listens intently to his confessional narrative. Colorful, fabric-covered props resembling flowers flank the stage, adding vibrant contrast, while the audience watches intently in silhouette, emphasizing the dramatic tension focused on the two central figures.

event_blocks.summary: On a brightly lit stage, the Second Performer in Red, clad in a long red robe, dramatically recounts a prophecy to the Woman in White Gown, who wears a flowing white gown, stating he would "blend with my own mother in bed and kill my father" and that he "fled" to avoid it, before addressing her as "my wife" and promising to "tell the truth." As the Second Performer in Red speaks with theatrical intensity and hand gestures, the Woman in White Gown responds with subtle movements and eventually reaches out to him as he bows his head and extends his hand. A low, sustained, ominous string sound swells as the stage lights dim dramatically, plunging the scene into near darkness illuminated by faint blue and green hues. The Second Performer in Red then moves to the center, approaching the Woman in Dark Clothing, who is blindfolded and in a hooded robe, initially with her back to the audience, placing a hand on her shoulder and continuing his monologue about encountering "an old man followed by an officer" at a "triple meeting of the roads." The Woman in Dark Clothing turns and raises her hands expressively during a solemn, ritualistic exchange as the Performer in Red gestures back, while the Performer with Staff remains mostly still and Background Performers observe near stylized props, and the Audience watches in silhouette, concluding the scene in near darkness with the central figures still engaged in their emotional exchange, underscored by somber music.

event_blocks.summary: In near-total darkness with faint blue stage lights, a deep male voice begins a dramatic monologue, declaring "furious, mad with rage" and recounting a violent encounter where a driver attempted to push him off the road, punctuated by a thud. As dim greenish light reveals the stage, the Woman in Dark Clothing stands center, with the Performer in Red and Performer with Staff nearby, while Background Performers observe near stylized tree and house props. The monologue continues, describing an old man swinging a walking stick, followed by a thud, and the speaker's retaliation with a "stick of my own, my spear!" The Woman in Dark Clothing turns and gestures, interacting with the Performer in Red. The lighting shifts to deep red as the Woman in Dark Clothing and two other performers adopt a ritualistic pose. The monologue culminates with "And I killed them all!" The Performer in Red moves left, where the Second Performer in Red, seated on a White Chair by a Stone-arch Prop, engages in dialogue with the Woman in White Gown, who wears a red headband and flowing gown with red trim. He responds with a bowed head and outstretched hand. Colorful Fabric Props frame the stage, and the Audience is visible in silhouette, with a Hand with Phone on Stick entering from the left. The Second Performer in Red's monologue ponders a connection to King Laius, concluding with regret, "then there is no one more sorry than me." The interaction ends with the Second Performer in Red and Woman in White Gown reaching toward each other before he walks away, leaving her by the Stone-arch Prop. Later, the Second Performer in Red, in a long red robe over a white tunic, seated on the White Chair, questions, "The hands that would bring about Laius's death, was it not so that my birth was cursed?" contemplating fleeing, never taking comfort, joining his mother in marriage, or killing his father, stating, "what little hope I have left rests with this man, this herdsman." The Woman in White Gown asks, "But when he come, what would you have for him?" and he replies, "If his words are consistent with yours, I might then escape disaster." She presses, "What was that you heard? That was so decisive?" and he begins to answer, "You said, according to this man's story." The lighting focuses on them, with Colorful Fabric Props framing the stage and the audience in silhouette. The Second Performer in Red states, "that there were soldiers who murdered the king. If his story stays the same, then I cannot be Laius's killer. Because simply, one man cannot be the same as many men," adding, "If he says there is a lone traveler, however..." The Woman in White Gown rises, places a hand on her chest, and challenges, "The city had him say those things, not I alone, but even if it says something else this time, it does not say that the death was properly fulfilled. Since the prophecy said that it will be his fate to die by the hands of his son. And yet, it was never that poor soul that killed him. He disappeared long time before." She moves behind him, placing a hand on his shoulder, and he leans into her touch, closing his eyes. She steps back, and he rises, clutching his chest, moving away, conceding, "You are... you are probably right." The lighting emphasizes their emotional connection. Finally, the Second Performer in Red, standing near the chair, instructs, "But just to be sure, send someone to bring the servant here, and do it quickly." The Woman in White Gown responds, "I will, my lord," and suggests, "But let us go inside the house for now." Soft, melancholic string music plays as they embrace tenderly, her whispering, "You know, there is nothing I wouldn't do to pleasure you." They pull back, maintain eye contact, and walk together toward the arch, exiting the stage as it fades to black, leaving faint blue stage lights and the continuing string music.

raw_segments: The video opens in near-total darkness, with only faint blue stage lights visible above, creating a mysterious and somber atmosphere. Simultaneously, a deep, resonant male voice begins a dramatic monologue, declaring, "furious, mad with rage," and describing how "their driver had attempted to push me" off the road, punctuated by a forceful thud. Gradually, a dim greenish light reveals a stage with several performers in period-style costumes. The Woman in Dark Clothing stands center stage with her back to the audience, while the Performer in Red and the Performer with Staff are positioned nearby. Background Performers stand near stylized tree and house props on the right, observing. The male voice continues, recounting a violent encounter: "When the old man saw this, he swung at my head with his walking stick," followed by another sharp thud, and his own retaliatory strike, "I swung at him with a stick of my own, my spear! Right to the ground he went!" The Woman in Dark Clothing turns and gestures expressively, interacting with the Performer in Red, who responds with subtle movements. The lighting shifts to a deep red, intensifying the emotional tone as the Woman in Dark Clothing raises her arms in a ritualistic pose, flanked by two other performers who mirror her gesture. The monologue culminates in a chilling declaration, "And I killed them all! I killed them all!" The Performer in Red then moves toward the left side of the stage, where the Second Performer in Red, seated on the White Chair beside the Stone-arch Prop, engages in a dramatic dialogue with the Woman in White Gown. She gestures with her hands, and he, the speaker of the ongoing monologue, responds with bowed head and outstretched hand, creating a moment of connection. The lighting brightens slightly, revealing more details: the Woman in White Gown wears a red headband and flowing gown with red trim, while the Second Performer in Red wears a long red robe over a white tunic. Colorful Fabric Props frame the stage, adding visual contrast. The Audience remains visible in silhouette, some turning their heads or raising hands, while a Hand with Phone on Stick enters from the left, capturing the scene. The Second Performer in Red's monologue continues, pondering a connection: "So yes, if there is a connection between this old man and King Laius..." and concluding with profound regret, "then there is no one more sorry than me." The interaction between the Second Performer in Red and the Woman in White Gown culminates in a tender moment as they reach toward each other, their hands nearly touching, before the Second Performer in Red turns and walks away, leaving the Woman in White Gown alone by the Stone-arch Prop.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage, focusing on the Second Performer in Red and the Woman in White Gown near the Stone-arch Prop. The Second Performer in Red, seated on the White Chair in a long red robe over a white tunic, engages in an intimate and expressive dialogue with the Woman in White Gown, who wears a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband. They hold hands, and he gently places his other hand on her shoulder, leaning in as he speaks with a dramatic male voice, questioning, "The hands that would bring about Laius's death, was it not so that my birth was cursed?" He continues, addressing her as "My wife," contemplating the grim possibilities of fleeing, never taking comfort, joining his mother in marriage, or killing his own father. His intense monologue persists as he states, "what little hope I have left rests with this man, this herdsman." The Woman in White Gown, reciprocating his hand-hold and shifting her stance slightly as she looks up at him, then responds with an inquisitive female voice, "But when he come, what would you have for him?" The Second Performer in Red replies, "If his words are consistent with yours, I might then escape disaster." She presses further, "What was that you heard? That was so decisive?" to which he begins to answer, "You said, according to this man's story," before the minute concludes. The lighting remains focused on them, casting soft shadows on the black backdrop, while Colorful Fabric Props frame the stage at the front, and the audience is visible in silhouette, observing this tender yet narratively significant exchange.

On a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, a gray Stone-arch Prop frames a white chair adorned with a small bouquet of flowers. The Second Performer in Red, dressed in a long red robe over a white tunic, sits on the chair, engaging in an intimate and emotionally charged dialogue with the Woman in White Gown, who wears a flowing off-the-shoulder gown with red trim and a red headband. He speaks with a serious, dramatic tone, stating, "that there were soldiers who murdered the king. If his story stays the same, then I cannot be Laius's killer. Because simply, one man cannot be the same as many men," continuing, "If he says there is a lone traveler, however..." The Woman in White Gown, initially seated beside him and listening intently, then rises, placing a hand on her chest in a gesture of emotion. She responds with a clear, serious voice, challenging his statements: "The city had him say those things, not I alone, but even if it says something else this time, it does not say that the death was properly fulfilled. Since the prophecy said that it will be his fate to die by the hands of his son. And yet, it was never that poor soul that killed him. He disappeared long time before." As she speaks, she moves behind him, gently placing her hand on his shoulder, and he leans into her touch, closing his eyes briefly. She then steps back, turning to face him with a look of concern or urgency. He rises from the chair, clutching his chest as if in pain or distress, and moves slightly away, while she watches him with a mix of worry and resolve. He then concedes, his voice tinged with distress, "You are... you are probably right." The lighting remains focused on the two, casting soft shadows and emphasizing the emotional weight of their evolving connection.

The scene opens with the Second Performer in Red, dressed in a long red robe over a white tunic, standing near a white chair beside the Stone-arch Prop. He engages in a quiet, emotional dialogue with the Woman in White Gown, who wears a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband. The Second Performer in Red, speaking with a clear male voice, instructs, "But just to be sure, send someone to bring the servant here, and do it quickly." The Woman in White Gown, initially standing with her back partially turned and arms crossed, responds in a soft female voice, "I will, my lord," and then adds, "But let us go inside the house for now." As soft, melancholic string music plays in the background, the Second Performer in Red gestures gently with his hands, appearing to speak earnestly. He steps closer, places his hand on her shoulder, and initiates a tender embrace. The Woman in White Gown reciprocates, turning toward him, and they share a close, intimate moment, their faces near, eyes closed, conveying deep emotion. During this embrace, she whispers, "You know, there is nothing I wouldn't do to pleasure you." The lighting remains focused on them, casting soft shadows against the dark backdrop, while the Stone-arch Prop looms behind them as a symbolic frame. After a moment of connection, they slowly pull back, maintaining eye contact, and then turn together toward the arch, walking away from the chair with audible footsteps and exiting the stage. The scene fades to black, leaving only faint blue stage lights visible in the darkness, as the string music continues to play, signaling the end of the sequence.

event_blocks.summary: In near darkness, a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop and colorful fabric props at the front is revealed, accompanied by gentle, melancholic string music, as a silhouetted audience watches. A Woman in White Gown, seated on a white chair near a stone-arch prop, is approached by a Woman in Dark Clothing who inquires about the house of Oedipus. The Woman in White Gown reveals the location and identifies herself as his wife, leading to an exchange where the Woman in Dark Clothing offers a blessing. Later, the Woman in White Gown and the Woman in Dark Clothing face each other in a tense exchange, with the Woman in White Gown questioning the origin of benefits. A Performer in Red enters, relaying that Oedipus will be made king in Corinth, and that his father, Polybus, is dead. The unidentified male voice of Oedipus expresses distress and outrage upon learning this news. The Performer in Red then declares that prophecies about coupling with his mother and killing his father are worthless, as his mother, Queen Merope, is alive. The Woman in White Gown clarifies that King Polybus was not his father, and that she received him as a gift from the Woman in Dark Clothing, who found him on the mountain with pierced feet. The Performer in Red, increasingly agitated, demands to know if this was his mother or father's work, to which the Woman in White Gown distressedly replies she cannot tell. The Woman in Dark Clothing states that another shepherd gave the Performer in Red to her, and he demands to know who this man is, learning he was one of Laius's herdsmen. The Woman in White Gown, seated, advises against pursuing this, but the Performer in Red insists on uncovering the truth and calls for the herdsman to be brought. The Woman in White Gown pleads with him not to uncover the truth, lamenting, 'You poor man. I hope you will never learn your mother's name. I grieve for you. Never, evermore.'

Audience.description: Silhouetted figures seated in rows of chairs facing the stage, visible in the foreground during the wider shot, observing the performance with some members turning their heads or raising hands in response to the action.

Performer in Light Clothing.description: A performer in light-colored, layered clothing, who briefly interacts with the Woman in Dark Clothing by placing a hand on her shoulder before stepping back, standing near the stylized tree and house props.

Woman in White Gown.description: A woman in a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, who stands near a white chair beside the Stone-arch Prop, holding a small book or box. She speaks with a pleading and sorrowful female voice, urging "If you care about your life here at all... Do not do this," and later lamenting, "You poor man. I hope you will never learn your mother's name. I grieve for you. Never, evermore." She receives a comforting touch and embrace from the Performer in Red, and later follows him and the Woman in Dark Clothing toward the Stone-arch Prop, briefly sitting on the White Chair before standing again.

Performer in Red.description: A performer in a long red robe over a white tunic, with short light-colored hair, who engages in an emotional dialogue with the Woman in White Gown, speaking with a confident, defiant, and authoritative male voice. He states, "Like I said, you need not be afraid, because even if my mother is a slave, your birth is still noble," and later declares, "You can never talk me out of uncovering the truth! Even if you mean well. So, let someone go bring the herdsman here." He places a hand on the Woman in White Gown's shoulder, embraces her, and later touches the Woman in Dark Clothing's shoulder, gesturing expressively throughout, before walking away with the Woman in Dark Clothing toward the Stone-arch Prop.
