raw_segments: The scene opens in near darkness, gradually revealing a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop and a few scattered stage lights, all framed by colorful fabric props resembling flowers at the front. Gentle, melancholic string music plays throughout the segment. The audience is visible in silhouette, seated in rows of chairs facing the stage. On stage, a Woman in White Gown, wearing a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, sits on a white wooden chair beside a gray stone-arch prop, holding a book. To her right, a group of Background Performers in earth-toned, period-style garments with head coverings stand near stylized tree and house props, observing. A Woman in Dark Clothing, in a dark, textured hooded robe, enters from the audience side, walking toward the center. She is briefly approached by a Performer in Light Clothing, who places a hand on her shoulder before stepping back. The Woman in Dark Clothing then addresses the stage, asking in a clear female voice, "My friends, might I inquire for one of you to tell me where's the house of Oedipus, the king? Or tell me where he is?" She then walks toward the Woman in White Gown. The Woman in White Gown stands, and they face each other, engaging in a quiet, emotional exchange. The Woman in White Gown, in a calm female voice, responds, "That is his house. That lady is his wife." The Woman in White Gown places a hand on the Woman in Dark Clothing’s shoulder as they share a moment of connection. The Woman in Dark Clothing then offers a blessing, stating, "May you forever be happy, and may you be happy forever with king as his perfect queen." The Woman in White Gown replies, "I wish you joy as well, my friend." As they walk together toward the stone-arch prop, the Woman in Dark Clothing asks, "So tell me, what is it that you want?" and immediately continues, "I came with something that will benefit both you..." The lighting remains focused on the central figures, creating a dramatic and intimate atmosphere.

The scene opens with the Woman in White Gown, adorned in a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, standing face-to-face with the Woman in Dark Clothing, in a dark, textured hooded robe, on stage in a tense, silent exchange. A clear female voice, belonging to the Woman in White Gown, questions, "and your husband. What kind of benefits? From where have you have come?" As the moment builds, the Performer in Red, wearing a long red robe over a white tunic, enters from the left and approaches the Woman in White Gown, placing a hand on her shoulder. A clear male voice, belonging to the Performer in Red acting as a messenger, responds, "I come from Corinth. The people there would make Oedipus king." The Woman in Dark Clothing steps back slightly, observing the interaction. The Woman in White Gown, responding with subtle shifts in posture and facial expression indicating inner turmoil, asks, "What does this mean? Is Oedipus no longer the ruler?" The Performer in Red replies, "Why no. Death is holding him firm." The Woman in White Gown then asks with disbelief, "Old man Polybus is dead, you say?" An unidentified male voice, filled with distress and seemingly representing Oedipus, exclaims, "My wife, my dear, what's happening? Who is this messenger and what does he say?" The Woman in White Gown, processing the news, states, "He came from Corinth," and then relays the crucial information: "And he came... he came to tell that your father is dead." The unidentified male voice (Oedipus) reacts with a pained "Oedipus!" and questions, "By evil plots or by disease?" The Performer in Red, the messenger, clarifies, "He died of old age, my lord." The unidentified male voice (Oedipus) then expresses outrage, "Then on whose authority was I convicted of killing my father? If he lies beneath the earth, and still I am here!" The Performer in Red steps back, and the Woman in White Gown turns toward the Woman in Dark Clothing, placing a hand on her shoulder in a gesture of connection. The two then walk together toward the Stone-arch Prop at the center-left of the stage, exiting the main performance area. The camera pulls back to reveal the full stage, showing the audience in silhouette, the Stylized Background Set with trees and a house, and the White Chair beside the Stone-arch Prop. The lighting remains focused on the central performers, creating a dramatic contrast with the dark surroundings, while a Hand with Phone on Stick briefly enters from the left, capturing the moment.

On a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, Colorful Fabric Props frame the front, and a Stylized Background Set of trees and a house is faintly visible. The Audience watches in silhouette. The Performer in Red, dressed in a long red robe, begins a dramatic emotional exchange, gesturing expressively toward the Woman in White Gown. He speaks with a dramatic male voice, declaring, "Innocent! These... these worthless prophecies. Must I still shrink in fear from my mother's bed?" The Woman in White Gown, in a flowing white gown, initially faces the stoic Woman in Dark Clothing. She responds with a female voice, advising, "You must not fear your mother's bed," and adds, "Many men have dreamed of sleeping with their mothers." The Performer in Red places a hand on the Woman in White Gown’s shoulder, engaging her in tense dialogue, stating, "I cannot be but full of dread, as my mother is still alive." The Woman in White Gown questions him, "Who is this woman you're so afraid of? And what is causing you this fear?" The Performer in Red reveals, "Queen Merope, dear messenger," addressing the Woman in White Gown, "I have seen a terrible prediction, you see." The Woman in White Gown then turns toward the Woman in Dark Clothing, places a hand on her shoulder, and they begin walking together toward the Stone-arch Prop, passing the White Chair adorned with flowers. As they move, the Woman in White Gown asks, "Can you speak of it, or is it forbidden for others to know?" The Performer in Red follows them, gesturing and speaking, revealing the prophecy: "I believe it is common knowledge that the prophecies once said I would couple with my own mother in bed and kill my father. This is why I for these long years have left my home far behind. I had no wish to be my father's murderer." The Woman in White Gown briefly sits on the White Chair before standing again to embrace the Woman in Dark Clothing, and they continue walking together toward the arch. The Woman in White Gown challenges his understanding, stating, "It is pretty clear that you have been wrongly informed. If you as a..." The Performer in Red remains near the chair, looking up and gesturing as if in farewell or reflection, as the two women exit.

The scene opens on a stage with a dark curtain, where the Performer in Red, in a long red robe, engages in an intense dialogue with the Woman in White Gown, who sits on a White Chair holding a book. The Performer in Red expresses his fear that a prophecy "might be true," to which the Woman in White Gown calmly questions if his "fear is needless." He asserts, "How can that be? I am my parents' child," but she reveals that "King Polybus had nothing to do with your creation." As he demands, "What do you mean?", she clarifies, "Polybus was not your father anymore than I am." The Performer in Red, gesturing expressively and his voice rising in anger, then turns and walks toward the Woman in Dark Clothing, who stands observing in a dark hooded robe. He shouts, "Then why did he name me his son!", his voice filled with fury. The Woman in White Gown explains that he "received you as a gift... from me," as the Performer in Red places a hand on the Woman in Dark Clothing’s shoulder. The Woman in Dark Clothing kneels before him, looking up with a mix of submission and emotion, while he leans down, placing his hand on her neck in a gesture suggesting authority or comfort. He questions, "Did you purchase me or did chance put me in your way?", his voice still emotional. The Woman in White Gown recounts finding him "up in the mountain" as a shepherd and freeing "the bolts that pierced your feet," identifying herself as the cause of his rescue. A Hand with Phone on Stick briefly enters from the left, capturing this dramatic moment. The Performer in Red, with increasing intensity, demands to know if this was his "mother or my father's work," to which the Woman in White Gown distressedly replies, "I cannot tell." He then shouts, "I demand you tell me!", as he walks away with the Woman in Dark Clothing, who rises and follows him, leaving the Woman in White Gown seated and watching. Stylized tree and house props are visible in the background, and colorful fabric props add vibrancy to the front of the stage, all observed by the silhouetted Audience.

The scene opens with the Performer in Red, clad in a long red robe over a white tunic, standing on a low platform and interacting with the Woman in Dark Clothing, who is kneeling before him in a gray hooded robe with brown arm guards. The Woman in Dark Clothing speaks with a strained, emotional voice, stating, "He who gave you to me better knows. Another shepherd gave you to me." The Performer in Red places a hand on her shoulder, and she rises, then kneels again as he steps back, gesturing expressively while demanding in a commanding, intense voice, "And who is he? Who is this man?" The Woman in Dark Clothing hesitantly replies that she heard he was "one of Laius's herdsmen, a shepherd to the king," and believes he is "the one that the countryman you already wish to see," suggesting, "Your wife might be the best to speak of this." She then rises and walks away from him toward the right side of the stage, passing a pile of colorful fabric props resembling flowers. The Performer in Red, with a thoughtful tone, repeats, "My wife?" and then questions, "You asked her? Do you know if this man is the one he meets?" As the camera pans left, the Woman in White Gown, wearing a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, is revealed seated on a white chair beside a stone-arch prop. She speaks with a calm, resigned voice, advising, "Why talk of him? Do not concern." The Performer in Red approaches her, and they engage in an emotional exchange, with him speaking with increasing urgency and determination about catching "the clues that illuminate my ancestry." She stands, places a hand on the Woman in Dark Clothing’s shoulder, and then embraces her, interrupting the Performer in Red with a distressed cry of "For God's sake!" Together, they walk toward the stone-arch prop, with the Performer in Red following them. The audience, visible in silhouette, watches intently, some turning their heads or raising hands. The background features stylized tree and house props, and the lighting remains focused on the central performers, creating a dramatic, intimate atmosphere.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, where three performers engage in a dramatic, emotionally charged interaction. The Woman in White Gown, adorned in a flowing white gown with red trim and a red headband, stands near a white chair beside the Stone-arch Prop, holding a small book or box. She initiates a pleading dialogue, urging in a sorrowful female voice, "If you care about your life here at all... Do not do this." She faces the Performer in Red, who wears a long red robe over a white tunic and has short light-colored hair. He responds with a confident, reassuring male voice, placing a hand on her shoulder and later embracing her, stating, "Like I said, you need not be afraid, because even if my mother is a slave, your birth is still noble." Meanwhile, the Woman in Dark Clothing, dressed in a gray cape over dark clothing with brown arm guards and laced boots, stands stoically to the right, observing the exchange, occasionally shifting her weight and looking down, appearing contemplative. The Woman in White Gown continues her emotional plea, "For the best... I beg of you... Do not do this," to which the Performer in Red defiantly declares, "You can never talk me out of uncovering the truth! Even if you mean well. So, let someone go bring the herdsman here." As the interaction intensifies, the Woman in Dark Clothing receives a brief touch on her shoulder from the Performer in Red, prompting her to kneel before him. She then rises and walks away with him toward the Stone-arch Prop, while the Woman in White Gown follows, briefly sitting on the White Chair before standing again, her voice filled with deep sorrow as she laments, "You poor man. I hope you will never learn your mother's name. I grieve for you. Never, evermore." The stage is framed by colorful fabric props resembling flowers at the front, and the audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground, some turning their heads or raising hands, with the lighting remaining focused on the central performers, emphasizing the emotional gravity of the scene.

event_blocks.summary: The Performer in Red and the Woman in White Gown engage in an emotional exchange near the Stone-arch Prop, with the Performer in Red exclaiming, "You poor wretch! You cursed! Come back!" and the Woman in White Gown expressing fear of an "storm of misfortune." The Performer in Red defiantly accepts the challenge, and as they exit through the arch, the Woman in Dark Clothing enters. The Performer in Red returns, facing the Woman in Dark Clothing, and declares his pursuit of the secret surrounding his birth, identifying her as a potential source of information. The Woman in Dark Clothing confirms she recognizes a herdsman and walks with the Performer in Red toward the Stone-arch Prop. The Performer with Staff enters and is questioned by the Performer in Red about his past service to Laius, which the Performer with Staff confirms. The scene shifts to a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop and rustic props, where the Performer in Red confronts the Woman in Dark Clothing about a child, accusing her of giving him away. The Woman in Dark Clothing confesses, "I did. And I wish I'd died that day," revealing the child was not hers. The Performer with Staff, observing from the right, explains that the child was from Laius's house, given to him by Jocasta to kill due to prophecies of him killing his father. The Performer with Staff reveals to the Performer in Red that if he is the child of Laius and Jocasta, he is damned. Throughout these exchanges, the Woman in Dark Clothing observes solemnly, and cameras on tripods capture the performance for the silhouetted audience.

raw_segments: The scene opens with the Performer in Red and the Woman in White Gown near the Stone-arch Prop, engaged in a quiet, emotional exchange. The Performer in Red's strong male voice exclaims, "You poor wretch! You cursed! Come back!" The Woman in White Gown, with a distinct female voice, expresses fear, stating, "I'm afraid the storm of misfortune is coming." The Performer in Red defiantly responds, "Let it come!" as she turns and walks toward the arch, followed by him placing a comforting hand on her shoulder. As they exit through the arch, the Woman in Dark Clothing enters from the right, standing stoically near center stage. The Performer in Red returns, now facing the Woman in Dark Clothing, and gestures expressively, reflecting on his ancestry: "But such is my ancestry and I will pursue the secret surrounding my birth." He then walks toward her, places a hand on her shoulder, and declares, "My friend, I do believe I see the herdsman I seek." The Woman in Dark Clothing, with a calm female voice, responds, "Yes, I recognize him. He was one of Laius's shepherds," as she kneels before him, then rises and walks with him toward the Stone-arch Prop. A new figure, the Performer with Staff, enters from the right, holding a long wooden staff, and approaches the pair. The Performer in Red turns to face him, gesturing as he calls out, "You there! Old man, come! Look me in the face and answer. Were you once one of Laius's men?" The Performer with Staff, with a steady male voice, confirms, "I was. For most of my life, I followed him," as the Woman in Dark Clothing stands beside them, and the trio remains in a tense, triangular formation. The dimly lit stage, with its black curtain backdrop and stylized tree and house props, creates a symbolic, rural atmosphere, enhanced by colorful fabric props resembling flowers at the front. The audience is visible in the foreground, silhouetted, with some members reacting, and a Hand with Phone on Stick briefly captures the performance from the left.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, where the Performer in Red, dressed in a vibrant red robe, engages in a dramatic, emotionally charged interaction with the Woman in Dark Clothing, who wears a gray cape. The Performer in Red, speaking with a clear, questioning male voice, asks, "Do you recognize him?" referring to a messenger. The Woman in Dark Clothing, listening intently, responds with a deeper, hesitant male voice, stating, "No. Not that I could remember." The Performer in Red then declares, "That's not a surprise, my lord," and promises to "refresh his memory," revealing they were once neighbors. The Woman in Dark Clothing acknowledges, "What you say is true. But it was a long time ago." The Performer in Red then directly confronts her, asking with an accusatory tone, "Do you remember giving me a child? This man right here was once that baby boy." The Woman in Dark Clothing reacts with a guttural exclamation, "God damn you, man!" but is immediately silenced by the Performer in Red, who authoritatively commands, "Hold your peace! Do not abuse the messenger, old man!" The Woman in Dark Clothing defiantly retorts, "But he speaks in ignorance." The Performer in Red presses further, demanding to know if she "did or did not give this child to this man." With a voice full of regret, she confesses, "I did. And I wish I'd died that day." The Performer in Red, now threatening, warns she "may still die today if you don't tell the truth," and demands, "Where did you get the child from?" She begins to reveal, "It was not mine. I got it from..." before the segment ends. Throughout this intense exchange, the Performer in Red places a hand on her shoulder, prompting her to kneel briefly before rising and walking away with him toward the right side of the stage. The Performer with Staff, clad in dark, form-fitting clothing and holding a long wooden staff, remains mostly stationary on the right, a silent, watchful presence, observing the entire exchange. A stylized, weathered green tree prop and a small house-like structure add a rustic element, with a small camera on a tripod visible near the tree, capturing the performance.

On a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, three performers engage in a dramatic interaction. The Performer with Staff, in dark laced clothing and holding a wooden staff near a stylized tree prop, speaks with a calm male voice, explaining that "Someone else... It was a child from Laios's house," and suggesting "his wife would tell you more." The Performer in Red, centrally located in a vibrant red robe over a white tunic, responds with an intense, questioning male voice, demanding, "Why should she? Who gave it to you?" The Performer with Staff clarifies, "It was, my lord. With the intent to kill the boy." The Performer in Red, gesturing expressively, presses further, "And why? Why would a mother do such a thing?" The Performer with Staff explains the motive, stating, "She was afraid of the prophecies. They said the boy would kill his own father," and elaborates on his own actions, "I thought my friend here would take the child elsewhere, away from his horrible destiny." As the camera slowly zooms in on their tense exchange, the Performer with Staff briefly places a hand on the Performer in Red’s shoulder, delivering a dramatic revelation: "If you really are the child of King Laios and Jocasta, you should know, you are damned." Throughout this, the Woman in Dark Clothing, in a gray cape and dark clothing, observes the dramatic interaction with a solemn, rigid posture from the left. A small camera on a tripod with a blue light captures the performance against the rustic stylized background, while the audience watches intently as silhouettes in the foreground.

event_blocks.summary: The Performer in Red, clad in a long red robe, animatedly speaks and gestures, declaring, "It has all turned out true!" beside the Woman in Dark Clothing, who observes with a serious expression in her gray cape. event_blocks.summary: On a dimly lit stage bathed in deep blue light, a performer in dark, laced clothing holding a wooden staff moves toward the center, declaring, "I do not think that even God could wash clean this house of its hidden crimes," while a woman in earth-toned garments with a basket observes. A performer in light clothing near a stylized tree prop asks, "Have you got any further news?" The staff performer then announces, "Our lady, Queen Jocasta, she now lies dead," prompting the question, "Poor lady, what happened?" The staff performer details her suicide, fleeing the house and cursing the marriage. Background performers in earth-toned garments observe near stylized tree and house props. The female voice asks again, "How did that matter end?" The staff performer, placing a hand on the light-clothed performer's shoulder, explains Oedipus's interference prevented witnessing her final act. A camera on a tripod is visible, and the audience reacts in silhouette. A narrator describes a man finding his wife "hung by the neck," then picking up a "golden pin" and striking his own eyes, crying out about "black rain gushed of blood." A clear female voice interjects, "Is this poor man's pain still bitter?" The narrator continues, stating the man "cursed things that I may not repeat, and demanded..." Colorful fabric props frame the stage. Later, a solemn male voice narrates, "He may be exiled from the land. He is weakened now and needs someone to guide his steps. Yet, he will show himself to you." Oedipus, in light clothing, kneels and crawls in distress, with the staff performer placing a hand on his shoulder. The woman with the basket expresses pity, "It is terrible to look upon the suffering of men. I grieve for you, Oedipus. I cannot dare to look at you, but still this horror, you inspire me." Oedipus cries out about "the darkness" and his misery, asking, "Where can I go with my pain?" and pleading, "Drive me to exile, my friends." The woman responds, "I pity you for your fate, my lord." Background performers observe near props, a camera captures the action, and an unoccupied white chair sits nearby. The audience reacts in silhouette. The scene shifts to near darkness with a spotlight on Oedipus, who writhes in anguish, crying out, 'I have come to be my father's murderer, and the groom of her who gave me birth,' and pleading, 'Hide me away outside the city, or kill me, kill me now!' A calm female voice states, 'Creon is present now.' Creon, in red, stands solemnly and replies to Oedipus's demand for exile, "I have not come to mock you, Oedipus, nor am I here to revenge your insults against me." Creon states such a decision is "for the oracles to decide." Oedipus requests a tomb for "the woman who lies within" and to "dwell amid the mountain range," then pleads with Creon to "take care of them" his daughters. The woman in dark clothing holds a wooden cross staff, flanked by background performers, one with a basket. Performers on the right hold wooden staffs. The audience reacts. Creon tells Oedipus, "You've had enough time for tears. Go now," and "Again, what you're asking me is for the oracles to answer." After Oedipus's acceptance, Creon instructs, "Come, and let your children go." Creon then proclaims to the audience, "Citizens of Thebes, observe this man. Observe the fortune he has plumed. Never call a man fortunate until his final day finally arrives. Never, until he is unhurt by grief and by pain. For to run after the truth, well, that may be the real freedom that we have."

raw_segments: The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage bathed in deep blue light, creating a somber and mysterious atmosphere. From the left, a performer in dark, laced clothing, holding a wooden staff, moves slowly toward the center, gesturing ritualistically. He speaks with a deep, dramatic male voice, declaring, "I do not think that even God could wash clean this house of its hidden crimes." Nearby, a woman in earth-toned garments carrying a woven basket stands still, observing. In the center, a performer in light-colored, layered clothing with a head covering stands near a stylized tree prop, facing the staff performer. This central performer, speaking with a concerned, slightly higher-pitched female voice, inquires, "Have you got any further news?" The staff performer then gravely announces, "Our lady, Queen Jocasta, she now lies dead." The female voice responds with distress, "Poor lady, what happened?" The staff performer continues his narrative, detailing how "She killed herself. She fled the house full of emotion and hurried over to her marriage bed," where "She cursed aloud the marriage which had burdened a husband from a husband, children from a child." To the right, a group of three background performers in similar earth-toned, head-covered garments stand near stylized tree and house props, observing the central interaction, with one briefly bending forward. The female voice again asks, "How did that matter end?" The performer with the staff moves closer to the performer in light clothing, placing a hand on their shoulder, and explains, "I, I do not know. Because Oedipus, he burst in and did not let us look upon her final act of suffering." A small camera on a tripod is visible near the tree prop, capturing the performance with a faint blue light on its front. The audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground, some turning their heads or raising hands in response to the unfolding drama, as the staff performer continues, "Back and forth he went..." The overall mood is tense and ritualistic, with slow, deliberate movements and a focus on symbolic gestures and positioning, underscored by the dramatic dialogue.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage bathed in deep blue light, creating a somber and mysterious atmosphere. A dramatic male voice, acting as a narrator, begins a tragic tale, describing a man's search for his wife, his "dreadful cry," and stumbling into a room. On stage, performers in period-style attire move slowly and deliberately. A performer in dark, laced clothing, holding a wooden staff, walks toward the center, gesturing with the staff. The narrator continues, revealing the man found his wife "hung by the neck." The performer with the staff then places a hand on the shoulder of a performer in light-colored, layered clothing with a head covering, who stands near a stylized tree prop, an action that resonates with the unfolding tragedy. To the left, a woman in earth-toned garments carries a woven basket, observing with a neutral expression, while another woman in dark clothing, draped in a gray cape with a gold sash, stands with arms crossed, watching with a serious demeanor. In the background, three performers in earth-toned garments with head coverings stand near stylized tree and house props, observing the scene, with one briefly bending forward. The narrator's intense storytelling continues, detailing how the man, lying on the ground, witnessed a "dreadful sight," then picked up a "golden pin," raised it, and "struck it through his own, open eyes," crying that he would never again see him nor the consequences of his crime. He vividly describes "black rain gushed of blood" at each strike. Suddenly, a clear female voice interjects, asking, "Is this poor man's pain still bitter?" The male narrator then resumes, stating the man "cursed things that I may not repeat, and demanded..." before the segment concludes. Colorful fabric props resembling flowers or foliage frame the front of the stage, adding visual contrast, and a small camera on a tripod, emitting a blue light, captures the performance. The audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground during wider shots, some turning their heads or raising hands, reacting to the unfolding, tense, and reflective ritualistic narrative.

The scene opens in near darkness under a cool blue light, gradually illuminating a stage adorned with colorful fabric props. A solemn male voice narrates, declaring, "He may be exiled from the land. He is weakened now and needs someone to guide his steps. Yet, he will show himself to you." On stage, a Performer with Staff in dark, laced clothing moves slowly to the center, where a Performer in Light Clothing, identified as Oedipus, kneels and crawls in distress. The Performer with Staff places a hand on Oedipus's shoulder. A Woman in Earth-Toned Garments with Basket observes, then speaks with concern, "It is terrible to look upon the suffering of men. I grieve for you, Oedipus. I cannot dare to look at you, but still this horror, you inspire me." Oedipus then cries out with gasps and grunts, "Ah! Ah!" before speaking with intense anguish about "the darkness" and his misery, asking, "Where can I go with my pain?" He continues, describing how "This pain, it has pierced me with memories of grief," and lamenting, "I met my fate, my friends. For what should I now need eyes? What is that for me to see? Left for me to love?" He then pleads, "Drive me to exile, my friends." The Woman in Earth-Toned Garments with Basket responds, "I pity you for your fate, my lord." Throughout, Background Performers in period-style garments observe near stylized tree and house props, a camera on a tripod captures the action, and an unoccupied white chair with flowers sits beside a stone-arch prop. The audience, visible in silhouette, reacts to the somber and symbolic performance.

The scene opens on a dimly lit stage with a black curtain backdrop, where a central performer in light-colored, layered clothing, adorned with white face paint and red facial markings, kneels and crawls on the floor, appearing distressed and writhing in anguish. Their voice, filled with intense despair, cries out, 'I have come to be my father's murderer, and the groom of her who gave me birth,' lamenting their existence and asking why they were not killed straight away. To their left, a solemn woman in dark, hooded clothing holds a wooden cross staff, while beside her, a woman in earth-toned garments carries a woven basket, both observing quietly. On the right, a woman in a white blouse and beige skirt with a head covering stands near stylized tree and house props, accompanied by another partially visible performer, with colorful fabric props resembling flowers framing the front left of the stage. As the scene progresses, the lighting shifts dramatically, plunging the stage into darkness, with a single spotlight isolating the central performer, emphasizing their profound isolation. The camera then pulls back to reveal the full stage and the audience in silhouette, as the other performers remain in the shadows, forming a silent, watchful semi-circle around the spotlighted figure. The central performer continues to writhe and gesture in anguish, their voice escalating as they declare, 'I am myself evil now,' and plead, 'Hide me away outside the city, or kill me, kill me now! Throw me away somewhere! You will never see me again, out at sea!' The powerful contrast between their frantic movement and the stillness of the others, along with the interplay of light and shadow, creates a somber and theatrical atmosphere, suggesting a moment of spiritual or emotional climax. A calm female voice then states, 'Creon is present now,' signaling a potential shift in the narrative.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a stark black backdrop, illuminated by focused spotlights that cast sharp shadows, emphasizing a theatrical, somber, and ritualistic mood. An initial off-screen female voice asks, "मिलती है कोई योर डिमांड्स" (Do you have any other demands?). At the forefront, the Performer in Light Clothing, identified as Oedipus, kneels and crawls on the stage floor, displaying distressed or prayerful movements, his white face paint and red facial markings accentuating his emotional state. He speaks with a deep, resonant male voice, questioning "Creon. Why should he give me credence now?" To the left, the Performer in Red, identified as Creon, stands solemnly, occasionally turning his head and shifting his gaze, and replies with a calm, authoritative male voice, stating, "I have not come to mock you, Oedipus, nor am I here to revenge your insults against me." Oedipus then urgently demands, "Then get me out of this land, as quickly as you can!" to which Creon calmly responds that such a decision is "for the oracles to decide." Oedipus then makes solemn requests, first for a tomb for "the woman who lies within," and then to "dwell amid the mountain range." His tone shifts to concern as he acknowledges Creon will "seek your own just Creon," then expresses confidence in his "true men" sons. However, his voice becomes vulnerable and intensely pleading when speaking of his "weaker creatures" daughters, begging Creon, "Please, oh please, you must take care of them... you are the only father left for them now," and asking him to "share what you save with them." In the center, the Woman in Dark Clothing holds a wooden cross staff, standing rigidly with a serious expression, flanked by three Background Performers in earth-toned garments with head coverings, one of whom holds a woven basket. These Background Performers remain mostly still, observing the scene with subtle shifts in posture. On the far right, a performer in dark clothing with a gray cloak and another in dark laced attire hold wooden staffs, standing motionless. The audience is visible in silhouette in the foreground, some turning their heads or raising hands, indicating engagement with the intense emotional drama.

The scene unfolds on a dimly lit stage with a stark black backdrop, illuminated by focused spotlights. At the forefront, the Performer in Light Clothing, identified as Oedipus, with white face paint and red facial markings, kneels and crawls on the stage floor, displaying a posture of distress. He speaks with a pleading, desperate male voice, asking, "But what are my terms of exile?" and later, "But they do not like me, Creon!" before desperately changing his mind, "No, no, wait! I've changed my mind. Leave them with me, please!" To the left, the Performer in Red, identified as Creon, stands solemnly, occasionally turning his head and shifting his gaze. He speaks with an authoritative, deep male voice, commanding Oedipus, "You've had enough time for tears. Go now," and dismissively adds, "Again, what you're asking me is for the oracles to answer." After Oedipus's resigned acceptance, "Fine. Then drive me away at once," Creon instructs, "Come, and let your children go." Following Oedipus's desperate plea, Creon declares, "Oedipus. The power that you used to have is gone." Creon then addresses the audience, visible in silhouette in the foreground, some turning their heads or raising hands, proclaiming, "Citizens of Thebes, observe this man. Observe the fortune he has plumed." He continues with a philosophical monologue, stating, "Never call a man fortunate until his final day finally arrives. Never, until he is unhurt by grief and by pain. For to run after the truth, well, that may be the real freedom that we have." Throughout, the Woman in Dark Clothing, holding a wooden cross staff, stands beside Creon, observing with a serious expression. Behind them, a group of Background Performers in earth-toned, period-style garments with head coverings stand near stylized tree and house props, observing; one bends forward slightly. On the far right, the Performer with Staff stands motionless, holding a wooden staff, maintaining a stoic posture. The Woman in Earth-Toned Garments with Basket stands still near the left side, holding a woven basket, her expression neutral. The consistent lighting casts sharp shadows, emphasizing the somber, ritualistic atmosphere of spiritual and emotional struggle.
